duration: On and off on the day reported
specific_location: 227 W Francisco St., La Veta, La Veta, CO
description: Perhaps the most similar "looking" bird to a Black Phoebe might be a Dark-eyed Junco-slate...at least in my mind. Both are quick, dark birds with light bellies. However, if one is familiar with the two birds, a quick look at the bill will tell a lot. The Black Phoebe's bill is thin, black, and sharp; while the Junco's bill is pink and thicker.   The next thing I discern is their respective bellies; the Phoebe's white comes up from its belly to a point while the Junco’s "line of demarcation" is horizontal. Is that "line" on the flycatcher called a vest; it rather looks like one. And the phoebe is somewhat smaller, I believe.  As to behavior; I watch Junco's feed on seed I scatter around bushes and plants. They are like chickens, scratching around looking for goodies. I don't know if Phoebes even eat seed; they always seem to be hawking bugs that fly near my pond. For several days I watched one Black Phoebe come to the upper "pond", to a particular rock. He'd hawk from there, taking quick sallies across the water or sometimes seem to jump straight up to catch a "fly-over".   As I may have mentioned, I often saw two and the day I reported I saw three Black Phoebes together at a single time. At first I wondered if they were young, but I guess not, as all were quite dark. The way the white feathers came up around their sides or low on their feet...they looked young and downy. I imagine they were just cold.Behaviors: As to behavior; I watch Junco's feed on seed I scatter around bushes and plants. They are like chickens, scratching around looking for goodies. I don't know if Phoebes even eat seed; they always seem to be hawking bugs that fly near my pond. For several days I watched one Black Phoebe come to the upper "pond", to a particular rock. He'd hawk from there, taking quick sallies across the water or sometimes seem to jump straight up to catch a "fly-over".Call: Sounds? I'm really bad with bird calls. I love the soft chuckles of the Juncos as they seem to always come in large groups that chat back and forth like chickens. The Phoebe, usually alone, has a high-pitched pip that sounds like Hip, Hip Hooray. It’s like two-part sound; two going up first (hip, hip), then going down for the second (hoo, ray)...one after the other. Perhaps I imagined one was calling his fledglings, as I do usually see them singly and the white on their sides seemed so wispy...rather line a youngster, I’d imagined.Plumage: unknown
photographer: Photographer: Beverly Jensen, Beverly Jensen, Beverly Jensen, Beverly Jensen, Beverly Jensen
